mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of the `throw` keyword in Java?

options.text: To explicitly throw an exception manually
To handle errors in your code
To ignore errors in your code
To prevent errors in your code

mcqs.explanation: The `throw` keyword is used to explicitly throw an exception manually, giving programmers control over error propagation in their applications.

mcqs.question: What is the syntax for throwing an exception in Java?

options.text: throw new ExceptionType("Error Message");
throw ExceptionType();
throw ExceptionType;
throw new ExceptionType;

mcqs.explanation: The correct syntax for throwing an exception in Java is `throw new ExceptionType("Error Message");`.

mcqs.question: What happens when an exception is thrown in Java?

options.text: The program continues executing
The program terminates
The program ignores the exception
The program handles the exception if caught

mcqs.explanation: When an exception is thrown in Java, the program terminates unless the exception is caught and handled appropriately.

mcqs.question: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java?

options.text: Checked exceptions are subclasses of RuntimeException, while unchecked exceptions are subclasses of Exception
Checked exceptions are subclasses of Exception, while unchecked exceptions are subclasses of RuntimeException
Checked exceptions are handled at runtime, while unchecked exceptions are handled at compile-time
Checked exceptions are handled at compile-time, while unchecked exceptions are handled at runtime

mcqs.explanation: Checked exceptions are subclasses of Exception (excluding RuntimeException and its subclasses), while unchecked exceptions are subclasses of RuntimeException or Error.

mcqs.question: Can you throw a custom exception in Java?

options.text: Yes, by extending the Exception class or one of its subclasses
No, custom exceptions are not allowed in Java
Yes, but only by extending the RuntimeException class
Yes, but only by extending the Error class

mcqs.explanation: Yes, you can throw a custom exception in Java by extending the Exception class or one of its subclasses.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the `throws` clause in Java?

options.text: To explicitly throw an exception manually
To declare exceptions that a method may throw
To handle errors in your code
To ignore errors in your code

mcqs.explanation: The `throws` clause is used to declare exceptions that a method may throw.

mcqs.question: What happens if you don't handle an exception that is thrown in Java?

options.text: The program continues executing
The program terminates
The program ignores the exception
The program enters an infinite loop

mcqs.explanation: If you don't handle an exception that is thrown in Java, the program terminates.
